ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 represents Chinese broccoli as a raw ingredient. The restaurant dish includes stir-frying with oil and oyster sauce; no exact cooked-dish code is available in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers the raw vegetable. The prepared stir-fried dish with oyster sauce is not separately coded; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 412061001 refers to the raw plant substance. The cooked restaurant preparation is not separately coded.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of stir-fried kai lan is approximately 200 g, representing a shared side dish for one to two persons.

nutrient_source.title: Chinese Broccoli (Gai Lan), cooked, boiled / stir-fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 11994 adapted with cooking-oil adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked Chinese broccoli (NDB 11994 / FDC ID 169967) used as the primary nutrient base. Values adjusted upward for energy, fat, and sodium to reflect restaurant-style stir-frying with approximately 5 g vegetable oil per 100 g cooked dish and oyster sauce seasoning, consistent with standard culinary practice. Protein and carbohydrate values retained from USDA cooked values. This is the best available approved-source anchor for this dish; HPB Singapore Foodo does not carry a direct entry for stir-fried kai lan with oyster sauce.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of non-starchy vegetables — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried kai lan with oyster sauce. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy with other non-starchy leafy green Brassica vegetables (e.g., broccoli GI ~10–15, cabbage GI ~10) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (15 × 4.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 1.35, rounded to 1.4. This is firmly in the low GI / low GL category.

hypertension.guidance: A 200 g restaurant serving of stir-fried kai lan contributes approximately 560 mg sodium, representing about 24% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried kai lan has a glycemic index of approximately 15 and only 4.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it an excellent vegetable choice for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary fibre, vitamin C, and folate with low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kai lan provides dietary fibre (2.2 g/100 g), folate (45 mcg/100 g), and vitamin C (45 mg/100 g) with low saturated fat (0.6 g/100 g), supporting cardiovascular health; the primary concern is sodium from oyster sauce, which should be moderated in patients with hypertensive cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried kai lan, also known as Chinese broccoli or gai lan (芥兰), is a classic Chinese and Singaporean vegetable dish made from Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra — a leafy green with thick, glossy dark-green leaves, firm edible stems, and small white flowers. In restaurant preparation, the vegetable is typically blanched briefly then stir-fried over high heat with garlic, vegetable oil, oyster sauce, and a touch of sesame oil, producing a dish with a slightly bitter, earthy flavour balanced by the savoury umami of oyster sauce. The texture is tender-crisp with a mild crunch in the stems. Kai lan is a nutritionally dense vegetable, providing vitamin C (~45 mg/100 g), folate (~45 mcg/100 g), calcium (~72 mg/100 g), dietary fibre (~2.2 g/100 g), and vitamin A (~48 mcg RAE/100 g). Its glycemic index is very low (estimated ~15), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The primary nutritional concern in restaurant preparations is sodium from oyster sauce, which can reach 280 mg/100 g or approximately 560 mg per standard 200 g serving. The dish is naturally cholesterol-free and low in saturated fat. It is a staple side dish in Chinese-Singaporean cuisine, commonly ordered alongside rice or noodle dishes, and is widely available at zi char (煮炒) restaurants, hawker centres, and Chinese restaurants across Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in stir-fried kai lan?
Is Chinese broccoli good for diabetics?

meal_context: Healthy vegetable option at Chinese restaurants

confidence_reason: No direct HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for stir-fried kai lan with oyster sauce as a prepared restaurant dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked Chinese broccoli data with a cooking-oil and sodium adjustment to reflect restaurant preparation. GI is estimated by analogy with non-starchy Brassica vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 169967, cooked Chinese broccoli) with an upward adjustment for fat (+~4 g/100 g) and sodium (+~230 mg/100 g) to reflect restaurant-style stir-frying with oil and oyster sauce. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based vegetable dish with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per USDA source. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived B12; naturally zero. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero for this leafy green vegetable; confirmed zero per USDA source. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish; naturally zero. 6. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on non-starchy Brassica vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for this dish exists. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and all B-vitamins are estimated from USDA cooked Chinese broccoli values and are reasonable approximations. 8. The dish is not suitable for strict vegans or vegetarians due to oyster sauce (mollusc-derived); halal status depends on oyster sauce certification. 9. Price SGD 23.40 from field survey input is preserved as context but not used in nutritional calculations. 10. Sentinel value -1 is not used in this record; all fields carry either measured/estimated numeric values or confirmed-zero values with justification above.
